skill.name: competitor-alternatives
skill.description: When the user wants to create competitor comparison or alternative pages for SEO and sales enablement. Also use when the user mentions 'alternative page,' 'vs page,' 'competitor comparison,' 'comparison page,' or 'vs page.'
skill.summary: Creates competitor comparison and alternative pages for SEO and sales enablement
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All 59 static findings are false positives from pattern matching on Markdown documentation. The detected 'external_commands' patterns are Markdown code examples showing URL patterns (not Ruby backticks), 'weak cryptographic' findings reference 'vs' in comparison page URLs (not cryptography), and 'system reconnaissance' is a benign architecture pattern. No executable code exists. The skill contains only documentation for creating competitor comparison pages.

low_findings.title: Static Analysis False Positives
low_findings.description: All 59 detected patterns are false positives from Markdown documentation. The 'external_commands' findings are Markdown code fence examples showing URL patterns like `/alternatives/[competitor]`, not executable Ruby backticks. The 'weak cryptographic algorithm' findings reference the word 'vs' (versus) in URL structure examples like `/vs/[competitor]`, not the vsencrypt cryptographic algorithm. The 'system reconnaissance' finding references 'single source of truth', a common software architecture pattern. This skill contains only documentation with no executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct examination of the file confirms all detected patterns are Markdown documentation examples, not executable code. URL patterns like `/vs/[competitor]` are standard documentation, not cryptography references.

content.user_title: Create Competitor Comparison Pages for SEO
content.value_statement: Capture high-intent search traffic from users researching competitors. This skill provides structured templates and methodologies for creating alternative pages, versus comparisons, and competitor roundups that convert.
actual_capabilities: Generate structured competitor comparison pages with TL;DR summaries
Create alternative-to-competitor pages that validate user pain points
Build versus comparison pages with feature-by-feature analysis
Design competitor-vs-competitor roundups that introduce your product
Centralize competitor data for consistent updates across all pages
Create index pages that organize all comparison content for SEO
limitations: Requires manual research to populate competitor data accurately
Does not automate the actual page generation or deployment
Depends on user having genuine knowledge of competitor offerings
Ongoing maintenance required to keep comparisons current

use_cases.title: SaaS Founder Creating Alternative Pages
use_cases.description: Founders of SaaS products use this skill to create 'Alternative to [Competitor]' pages that capture users actively looking to switch from competitors. The skill helps structure pages that validate pain points and position their product as the solution.

use_cases.title: Marketing Team Building Comparison Content
use_cases.description: Marketing teams use this skill to scale their competitor comparison content strategy. The centralized data architecture ensures consistency across dozens of comparison pages while the templates maintain quality and depth.

use_cases.title: SEO Specialist Capturing Competitor Traffic
use_cases.description: SEO specialists use this skill to capture high-intent search traffic from users researching competitors. The four page formats (alternative, alternatives, vs, competitor vs competitor) provide comprehensive coverage of competitive search terms.

prompt_templates.title: Create Single Competitor Alternative Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an alternative page for [Your Product] vs [Competitor]. Include: why people look for alternatives from [Competitor], quick positioning summary, detailed feature comparison, pricing breakdown, who should switch, migration path, and social proof from switchers. Use an empathetic tone for frustrated users.
prompt_templates.scenario: When you need to target users actively looking to switch from a specific competitor

prompt_templates.title: Create Competitor Alternatives Roundup
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a 'Best [Competitor] Alternatives' roundup page. Include: common pain points with [Competitor], evaluation criteria for alternatives, list of 4-7 real alternatives (including [Your Product]), comparison table, detailed breakdown of each option, and recommendations by use case. Position [Your Product] as one option among several.
prompt_templates.scenario: When targeting users in early research phase comparing multiple options

prompt_templates.title: Create Direct Versus Comparison
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a '[Your Product] vs [Competitor]' comparison page. Include: TL;DR summary, at-a-glance comparison table, detailed comparison by category (features, pricing, support, ease of use, integrations), who each product is best for, customer testimonials from switchers, and migration support. Be confident but fair—acknowledge where [Competitor] excels.
prompt_templates.scenario: When users are directly comparing your product to a specific competitor

prompt_templates.title: Create Competitor-vs-Competitor Roundup
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a '[Competitor A] vs [Competitor B]' comparison page that introduces [Your Product] as a third option. Include: overview of both competitor products, comparison by category, who each is best for, introduce [Your Product] as the alternative, three-way comparison table. Use an objective analyst tone to earn trust.
prompt_templates.scenario: When capturing search traffic for users comparing two competitors you're not directly competing with yet

output_examples.input: Create an alternative page for our project management tool vs Asana
output_examples.output: Generated a complete Asana alternative page with: pain point validation (Asana complexity, learning curve), feature comparison table (task management, views, automation), pricing breakdown ($10.99 vs your pricing), who should switch section (teams wanting simplicity), migration guide (CSV import, timeline expectations), and social proof section structure. The page emphasizes simplicity and faster onboarding as key differentiators.

output_examples.input: Build a vs page comparing our tool to Notion
output_examples.output: Created a comprehensive vs page including: TL;DR summary positioning your tool as focused databases vs Notion's all-in-one approach, comparison table across 6 categories, detailed feature paragraphs explaining tradeoffs, pricing comparison highlighting value differences, honest assessment of when to choose Notion (documentation-heavy teams), when to choose your tool (data-heavy workflows), and migration support details.

output_examples.input: Create a roundup of top Monday alternatives
output_examples.output: Produced a Monday alternatives roundup featuring 6 tools including yours. Structured with: common Monday pain points (over-complexity, notification overload), evaluation framework (ease of use, automation depth, pricing), detailed analysis of each alternative with pros/cons, comparison table, and recommendations by use case (e.g., 'Choose ClickUp for power users', 'Choose [Your Product] for teams wanting simplicity').

best_practices: Be honest about competitor strengths and your limitations—readers are comparing and will verify claims, so accuracy builds trust
Go beyond feature checkmarks by explaining why differences matter with specific use cases and scenarios
Centralize competitor data in structured files so updates propagate across all comparison pages automatically
Include 4-7 real alternatives in roundup pages rather than just competitors—being genuinely helpful ranks better
anti_patterns: Don't misrepresent competitor features or pricing—users will discover the deception and lose trust in your brand
Avoid superficial checkmark comparisons that don't explain the practical difference between features
Don't create comparison pages and never update them—competitor changes will make your content inaccurate and hurt credibility
Resist the urge to bash competitors—empathize with user pain points while positioning your product as the solution

faq.question: What types of competitor pages does this skill help create?
faq.answer: Four formats: (1) [Competitor] Alternative for users actively switching, (2) [Competitor] Alternatives (plural) for users researching options, (3) You vs [Competitor] for direct comparisons, and (4) [Competitor A] vs [Competitor B] to capture traffic and introduce yourself.

faq.question: How do I ensure my competitor pages are accurate?
faq.answer: Follow the deep research process: sign up for competitor trials, use the product yourself, check current pricing directly, mine reviews on G2/Capterra for common complaints and praise themes, and talk to customers who switched from competitors.

faq.question: How often should I update competitor comparison pages?
faq.answer: Quarterly checks for pricing and major feature changes are recommended. Also update when customers mention competitor changes, and do a full refresh of all competitor data annually.

faq.question: Should I be honest about competitor strengths?
faq.answer: Yes. Honesty builds trust. Acknowledge where competitors excel and where your product has limitations. Readers are actively comparing and will verify claims. Being fair and accurate makes your positioning more credible.

faq.question: How many alternatives should I include in roundup pages?
faq.answer: Include 4-7 real alternatives including your product. Being genuinely helpful by including legitimate options builds trust, ranks better in search results, and positions you as an objective resource rather than just self-promotional.

faq.question: What is the centralized data architecture mentioned in this skill?
faq.answer: Create structured data files for each competitor with pricing, features, strengths, weaknesses, and positioning. All comparison pages pull from this single source of truth, so updating competitor pricing once propagates across all pages automatically.
